instruction: Rewrite the content to meet insurance regulatory and compliance standards.

input.draft_text:
Direct Tax
In India, a direct tax is paid by an individual or a firm to the government. These taxes are paid directly by the taxpayers to the government and are called direct taxes. The taxpayers are individuals and companies, and these taxes are imposed directly by the government and are non-transferrable to any other entity1.
The Central Board of Direct Taxes, or CBDT, is the statutory body under the Central Board of Revenue Act, 1963, responsible for managing direct tax in India2. CBDT looks after the administration of direct tax laws and provides recommendations and inputs for planning direct taxes in India. Today, we will discuss direct tax meaning, its types and its benefits in detail.
What is Direct Tax?
A type of tax where the incidence and impact of taxation fall on the same entity, whether an individual or a company, is called a direct tax1. A direct tax is imposed depending on a taxpayer’s income, meaning the higher the income, the higher the tax rate applicable3. A common example is income tax slab rates. The rules for direct taxes are designed to distribute money in the country by collecting taxes from people depending on their paying capacity. Hence, direct taxes are an important source of revenue for the government, which is utilised to fund many public welfare services and goods.
Types of Direct Taxes4: There are many different types of direct taxes imposed by the government. Some of the common types of direct taxes are given below.
Income Tax 4- As the name suggests, it is the tax applicable to a taxpayer's income. Any individual with income above the basic exemption limit must pay tax as per the applicable tax rate during a financial year. Tax rates are applicable depending on the income bracket of the taxpayer. The government decides these rates and income brackets. Failure to pay income tax results in penalties or even imprisonment.
Wealth Tax4 - This tax applies to the net wealth of an individual or a company above a threshold. Net wealth is the total asset after deducting the liabilities. Wealth tax depends on the market value of the assets owned by a taxpayer. A property owner is compulsorily required to pay a wealth tax even if the property does not earn any income. Please note that with effect from April 1, 2016, the wealth tax has been abolished6.
Corporate Tax4 This is a direct tax levied on the profits earned by a corporate body. In India, for the purpose of taxation, companies are categorised as domestic and foreign. The corporate tax rates are applied based on a slab rate system which depends on the type of corporate body and the revenue earned by the corporate body.
Securities Transaction Tax4 - This tax is levied on transactions of securities listed on stock exchanges in India, such as stocks, bonds and mutual funds. The tax rate depends on the type of security traded and the nature of the transaction, whether sale or purchase.
Gift Tax5This tax is levied on the gifts received by a taxpayer. For taxation, a gift can be a monetary gift, a gift of moveable property, or a gift of immovable property. The tax rate depends on the type of gift and its value. If the total value of gifts received during the year exceeds Rs. 50,000, then sum of all such gifts received during the year will be charged to tax.
Capital Gain Tax4 - This tax is levied on the gain arising from the transfer of capital assets. The capital asset can be property, shares, bonds, ULIPs or any valuable material. Income from capital gains can be termed STCG, i.e. short-term capital gain and LTCG, i.e. long-term capital gain, based on the holding period. If the holding period is more than 12 months, LTCG
Impact of LTCG on ULIPs as per the new amendment
Let us understand how LTCG applies to a ULIP with a lock-in period of 5 years. In the 2021 Budget, ULIPs were added to the category of capital assets. According to this new amendment, a high-value ULIP, with a annual premium of more than ₹ 2.5 lakhs, will be taxed as capital gain if purchased on or after February 1, 2021. A long-term capital gains tax is applicable at applicable rate on the maturity/surrender/withdrawal value depending on investment in equity oriented or debt oriented ULIPs. However, ULIPs purchased before this date is tax-free subject to satisfaction of conditions as mentioned in Section 10(10D). The only exception to this case is the death proceeds received by the nominee.
Why are Direct Taxes important?
Direct taxes are important as they are beneficial for nation-building.
These taxes can maintain an economic and social balance because the taxes are applied according to a taxpayer's income, reducing income inequalities.
Taxes also help restrain rising inflation by reducing demand for goods and services.
With an increasing workforce, the return on direct taxes also increases, thus generating more revenue for the government.
These taxes enforce the equal distribution of wealth because people with high resources are required to make high contributions which are utilised to uplift the underprivileged sections of society.
These taxes also enforce transparency as they are directly paid by the taxpayer to the government and thus play an essential role in the economy.
Conclusion
Direct tax laws have been framed to fulfil the requirements of a growing economy without having to make amendments. To establish an efficient and effective direct tax system, a Direct Tax Code was drafted to replace the old Indian Income Tax Act of 1961. This code aims to simplify voluntary compliance and improve the tax-GDP ratio. The direct tax code is a well-organized and well-planned code for taxation that implements
A unified reporting system
A flexible system to accommodate all changes
A system that eliminates misuse and misinterpretation.
It's important to note that tax laws and policies in India are subject to change, so it's always a good idea to consult with a tax professional to understand the various types of direct taxes and tax implications of various financial products.
Sources
1https://economictimes.indiatimes.com/definition/direct-tax
2https://incometaxmumbai.gov.in/about-us/organisation-and-functions/cbdt/
3https://cleartax.in/g/terms/direct-tax
4https://blog.saginfotech.com/definition-and-list-of-direct-taxes-in-india
5https://incometaxindia.gov.in/Tutorials/18.%20Tax%20treatment%20of%20gifts.pdf
6https://incometaxindia.gov.in/Tutorials/41.%20Wealth_Tax.pdf

Direct Tax
In India, a direct tax is paid by an individual or a firm to the government. These taxes are paid directly by the taxpayers to the government and are called direct taxes. The taxpayers are individuals and other legal entities like companies, limited liability partnerships etc, and these taxes are imposed directly by the government and are non-transferrable to any other entity1. The Central Board of Direct Taxes, or CBDT, is the statutory body under the Central Board of Revenue Act, 1963, responsible for managing direct tax in India2. CBDT looks after the administration of direct tax laws and provides recommendations and inputs for planning direct taxes in India. Today, we will discuss direct tax meaning, its types and its benefits in detail. What is Direct Tax?  Corporate Tax4 This is a direct tax levied on the profits earned by a corporate body. In India, for the purpose of taxation, companies are categorised as domestic and foreign. This tax is levied on transactions of securities listed on stock exchanges in India, such as stocks, bonds and mutual funds. The tax rate depends on the type of security traded and the nature of the transaction, whether sale or purchase. Impact of LTCG on ULIPs as per the new amendment
Let us understand how LTCG applies to a ULIP with a lock-in period of 5 years. According to e Budget 2021, a high-value ULIP, with aggregate annual premium of more than ₹ 2.5 lakhs, will be taxed as capital gain if purchased on or after February 1, 20216. A long-term capital gains tax is applicable at applicable rate on the maturity/surrender/withdrawal value depending on investment in equity oriented or debt oriented ULIPs. However, ULIPs purchased before this date is tax-free subject to satisfaction of conditions as mentioned in Section 10(10D) of Income Tax Act 1961.
